clauses.recommendation: Prepare model release forms for event attendees to sign, or have signage stating that attendance constitutes consent for photography. Consult legal counsel on proper consent procedures.

clauses.finding: Cancellation within 7 days of event requires full $2,200 payment; earlier cancellation forfeits $500 deposit
clauses.explanation: The 7-day window is very short and could catch you by surprise. If the event is cancelled or postponed less than a week out for any reason (weather, emergency, low attendance), you owe the full fee even with no service rendered.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate a longer cancellation window (14-30 days is more common) or add force majeure provisions allowing postponement without full payment in case of emergencies.

clauses.finding: Photographer's liability capped at refund of fees paid if they totally fail to deliver
clauses.explanation: If the photographer no-shows, delivers corrupt files, or provides unusable images, your only remedy is getting your $2,200 back. You cannot recover costs for rebooking, lost marketing value, or any consequential business damages.
clauses.recommendation: Request higher liability cap or add performance guarantees. Consider requiring proof of professional liability insurance and backup equipment/photographer provisions.

clauses.finding: Contract governed by Florida state law
clauses.explanation: Any disputes will be resolved under Florida law. This is only problematic if your business is located far from Florida, as it may increase legal costs if disputes arise.
clauses.recommendation: If you are not in Florida, consider negotiating for your home state's law or adding an arbitration clause to reduce litigation costs.

red_flags: You retain no copyright ownership despite paying for the work - photographer keeps all rights
Extremely short 7-day cancellation window requiring full payment even if event is cancelled
You are solely responsible for obtaining consent from all photographed individuals, creating legal exposure
Liability capped at mere fee refund with no coverage for consequential damages or business losses
Cannot resell or sublicense images without additional photographer approval and fees
top_recommendation: Before signing, negotiate three critical changes: (1) extend the cancellation window to at least 14-30 days, (2) clarify and potentially broaden the usage license to cover all anticipated business needs, and (3) establish a clear process for obtaining model releases with photographer cooperation or include language that photography constitutes consent.

plain_english_summary: This is a 30-day excavator rental for $4,500/month where the renter (Lessee) bears all responsibility for damage, loss, theft, insurance, and must indemnify the owner against all claims. The owner has no liability for equipment failure or downtime, and late returns cost double the daily rate.

clauses.finding: Lessee bears all risk of loss, theft, or damage from delivery until return and must insure equipment for full value
clauses.explanation: You are responsible for any damage or loss even if it's not your fault (fire, theft, vandalism, etc.), and you must obtain insurance at your own expense
clauses.recommendation: Confirm you can obtain adequate insurance coverage before signing and factor insurance costs into your budget

clauses.finding: Lessor has no liability for any loss or downtime caused by equipment failure
clauses.explanation: If the excavator breaks down, you still pay rent and the owner owes you nothing for project delays or lost income, even if the failure was due to a pre-existing defect
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate for rental credit or reduced fees if equipment fails due to owner's poor maintenance or pre-existing defects

clauses.finding: Lessee indemnifies Lessor against all claims arising from Lessee's use of the equipment
clauses.explanation: You must defend and pay for any lawsuits related to your use of the equipment, including injury claims from third parties, which could be very costly
clauses.recommendation: Ensure your general liability insurance covers rental equipment operations and consider adding the Lessor as additional insured

clauses.finding: Late returns charged at 200% of the daily rate
clauses.explanation: If you return the equipment even one day late, you pay double the normal daily rate (approximately $300/day instead of $150/day)
clauses.recommendation: Build buffer time into your project schedule or negotiate more reasonable late fees (e.g., 150% or same-day rate)

clauses.finding: Lessee responsible for routine maintenance and all damage beyond normal wear and tear
clauses.explanation: You must perform oil changes, greasing, and other routine upkeep during the rental period and pay for any damage you cause
clauses.recommendation: Inspect equipment thoroughly before accepting delivery and document pre-existing condition with photos

clauses.finding: $4,500 monthly rental fee plus $2,000 refundable security deposit
clauses.explanation: Standard pricing structure with security deposit to cover potential damages; deposit should be returned if equipment is in good condition
clauses.recommendation: Clarify in writing what constitutes acceptable condition for full deposit refund and timeline for return of deposit

clauses.finding: Governed by the laws of the State of Arizona
clauses.explanation: Any disputes will be resolved under Arizona law, which may require you to litigate in Arizona courts
clauses.recommendation: Confirm this is acceptable for your location; if you're far from Arizona, consider negotiating for your home state

red_flags: Lessee bears 100% of risk for loss, theft, or damage regardless of fault
Owner has zero liability for equipment failure or your resulting losses/downtime
Extremely high late return penalty at 200% of daily rate
Broad indemnification with no carve-outs for owner's negligence
No warranty that equipment is in working condition or fit for purpose
top_recommendation: Before signing, obtain comprehensive equipment insurance coverage and negotiate to add exceptions to the limitation of liability clause so you receive rental credits if equipment fails due to owner's poor maintenance or pre-existing defects

plain_english_summary: This is an extremely one-sided franchise agreement that heavily favors the franchisor. The franchisee faces mandatory weekly payments regardless of profits, personal liability guarantees, one-sided termination rights, severe restrictions on operations and exit, and has waived important legal protections including jury trial rights.

clauses.finding: 9% royalty on gross sales plus 4% marketing fee payable weekly regardless of profitability, plus $45,000 initial fee
clauses.explanation: Payment based on gross sales (not profit) means you owe money even when losing money. Weekly payment frequency creates constant cash flow pressure. Combined 13% of gross revenue is very high.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to tie payments to net profitability or at minimum change to monthly payments. Request cap on fees during startup period or low-revenue months.

clauses.finding: Franchisor can terminate for dozens of defaults with no cure period; franchisee has essentially no termination right
clauses.explanation: This gives the franchisor absolute power to end the relationship instantly for minor issues while you are locked in for 10 years. You could lose your entire investment with no warning or ability to fix problems.
clauses.recommendation: Demand cure periods of at least 30 days for non-critical defaults and reciprocal termination rights. Negotiate early termination options with reasonable buyout terms.

clauses.finding: 3-year non-compete within 30 miles after termination for any similar business
clauses.explanation: Even after the franchise ends, you cannot use your industry experience to earn a living in your local area for 3 years. This could destroy your career and livelihood, especially if franchisor terminates you.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to reduce to 1 year and 10 miles, or eliminate entirely. Ensure non-compete does not apply if franchisor terminates without cause.

clauses.finding: All owners personally guarantee all obligations and royalty payments for full 10-year term
clauses.explanation: Your personal assets (home, savings, retirement accounts) are at risk for business debts. If the franchise fails, you remain personally liable for all payments for the entire 10 years.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to limit personal guarantee to first 2-3 years or cap at a specific dollar amount. Consider forming an LLC and try to eliminate personal guarantee entirely.

clauses.finding: Must purchase all supplies from franchisor-approved vendors at franchisor-set prices; must follow operations manual as amended at any time
clauses.explanation: Franchisor controls your costs completely and can change requirements anytime, potentially making your business unprofitable. You have no ability to source cheaper alternatives or control expenses.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate the right to use alternative vendors if prices exceed market rates by more than 10%. Request that manual changes require 90 days notice and cannot materially increase costs.

clauses.finding: Cannot sell or transfer without franchisor consent plus 5% transfer fee; franchisor has right of first refusal
clauses.explanation: You cannot exit the business or recoup your investment without franchisor approval. The 5% fee reduces your sale proceeds, and right of first refusal means franchisor can block any sale by matching the offer.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to eliminate or reduce transfer fee to 1-2%. Require franchisor to exercise right of first refusal within 30 days with same terms as buyer offer.

clauses.finding: Governed by franchisor's home state law; franchisee waives right to jury trial
clauses.explanation: You must litigate in franchisor's state under their favorable laws, increasing your costs. Waiving jury trial means a judge (not peers) decides disputes, typically favoring sophisticated corporate parties.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate for neutral venue or your home state. Remove jury trial waiver entirely - this is a fundamental legal right you should not surrender.

clauses.finding: 10-year term with highly restricted termination rights for franchisee
clauses.explanation: You are locked into a decade-long commitment with personal guarantees and weekly payments, but franchisor can terminate you anytime. This extreme imbalance traps you in a potentially unprofitable business.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate for 5-year initial term with renewal options, or include performance-based exit clauses allowing termination if revenue targets are not met after first 2 years.

red_flags: Personal guarantee for 10-year term puts all personal assets at risk indefinitely
Franchisor can terminate instantly for numerous reasons with no cure period while franchisee is locked in
Weekly royalty payments on gross sales (not profit) regardless of business profitability
3-year non-compete prevents earning a living in the industry after termination
Franchisor controls all supply pricing with no market rate protection
Jury trial waiver and franchisor's home state jurisdiction eliminates legal protections
No reciprocal rights - every clause favors franchisor with no balance
Right of first refusal and transfer restrictions make exit nearly impossible
top_recommendation: Do not sign this agreement as written. Consult a franchise attorney immediately to negotiate major revisions, particularly eliminating the personal guarantee, adding cure periods to termination clauses, reducing the non-compete, and establishing mutual termination rights. If franchisor refuses to negotiate these fundamental terms, walk away - this deal is structured for franchisee failure and franchisor profit regardless of your business performance.

plain_english_summary: This is a standard independent contractor agreement for graphic design services at $65/hour with monthly invoicing. The contractor gives up all rights to their work and must defend the company against any claims, including employment misclassification issues. The agreement can be terminated by either party with 15 days notice.

clauses.finding: All work product, including pre-existing materials incorporated into the work, automatically belongs to the Company as work-made-for-hire
clauses.explanation: This is overly broad for a contractor. The clause transfers not only the new work but also any pre-existing materials the contractor brings to the project, meaning the contractor loses ownership of their own prior creations, templates, or tools they may want to reuse for other clients.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to limit IP assignment to only the final deliverables created specifically for this project, excluding contractor's pre-existing materials, tools, and general methodologies.

clauses.finding: Contractor must indemnify Company for any third-party claims arising from the work OR from employment misclassification
clauses.explanation: This forces the contractor to pay the company's legal costs if the IRS or state labor board decides the contractor should have been classified as an employee. This shifts a business risk that is largely controlled by how the company manages the relationship onto the contractor.
clauses.recommendation: Remove or strongly push back on the misclassification indemnity provision. Contractor should only indemnify for third-party IP claims directly related to deliverables, not employment classification disputes.

clauses.finding: Hourly rate of $65 with monthly invoicing and net 30 payment terms
clauses.explanation: Standard payment structure for contractor work. Net 30 means payment could be up to 60 days out (30 days to invoice + 30 to pay), which may impact cash flow.
clauses.recommendation: Consider negotiating net 15 terms or requiring deposits for larger projects to improve cash flow.

clauses.finding: Contractor must keep Company information confidential for 3 years
clauses.explanation: A 3-year confidentiality period is reasonable and standard for protecting business information. The timeframe is limited rather than perpetual.
clauses.recommendation: Acceptable as written. Ensure you understand what constitutes confidential information and maintain appropriate safeguards.

clauses.finding: Either party can terminate with 15 days notice; Company pays for approved work completed
clauses.explanation: This is a balanced termination clause that gives both parties flexibility. The 15-day notice period provides some planning time, and the contractor is protected by being paid for completed work.
clauses.recommendation: Acceptable. Consider clarifying what constitutes 'approved work' to avoid payment disputes upon termination.

clauses.finding: Contractor receives no employee benefits and handles own taxes
clauses.explanation: This is standard for independent contractor relationships and correctly reflects the tax treatment difference between contractors and employees.
clauses.recommendation: Ensure you set aside approximately 25-30% of income for self-employment taxes and consider your own health insurance and retirement planning.

clauses.finding: Contract disputes will be resolved under Minnesota state law
clauses.explanation: Minnesota law will govern any disputes. This is only a concern if you are located far from Minnesota, as you may need to defend claims there.
clauses.recommendation: If you are not in Minnesota, consider requesting governing law in your home state or adding a provision for remote dispute resolution.

red_flags: IP assignment includes contractor's pre-existing materials, potentially losing rights to your own prior work and tools
Indemnification for employment misclassification shifts company's legal risk to contractor for issues largely outside contractor's control
No limitation of liability cap, meaning contractor has unlimited financial exposure for claims
top_recommendation: Do not sign without negotiating the IP Assignment clause to exclude your pre-existing materials and the Indemnification clause to remove misclassification liability. These two provisions create significant long-term risk to your business assets and financial security.

plain_english_summary: This is a data processing agreement where the Provider handles personal data on behalf of the Customer. The agreement has reasonable data protection provisions including breach notification and deletion rights, but applies the liability caps from the main contract to data breaches, which could significantly limit recovery for potentially serious data incidents.

clauses.finding: Customer is designated as data controller, Provider as data processor processing only on documented instructions
clauses.explanation: Clear role definition is essential for GDPR compliance and ensures Provider cannot use your data for their own purposes
clauses.recommendation: Ensure the main agreement clearly documents all processing instructions and permitted purposes

clauses.finding: Provider must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures including encryption and access controls
clauses.explanation: Security requirements are mentioned but lack specific standards or audit rights. No mention of compliance certifications like ISO 27001 or SOC 2
clauses.recommendation: Request detailed security documentation and annual audit rights, and specify required certifications in an appendix

clauses.finding: Provider may engage sub-processors with 30 days notice; Customer may object on reasonable grounds
clauses.explanation: Standard GDPR-compliant provision allowing you to vet third parties who will access your data, though 30 days may be tight for full due diligence
clauses.recommendation: Request a current list of all sub-processors before signing and consider negotiating approval rights rather than just objection rights

clauses.finding: Provider must notify Customer of personal data breaches within 72 hours of becoming aware
clauses.explanation: Aligns with GDPR's 72-hour notification requirement to authorities, giving you time to meet your regulatory obligations
clauses.recommendation: Ensure notification includes all details required by GDPR Article 33 (nature of breach, affected data subjects, likely consequences, mitigation measures)

clauses.finding: Liability under this DPA is subject to the limitation of liability in the main agreement, which may cap data-related claims
clauses.explanation: Data breaches can result in massive regulatory fines and damages far exceeding typical service fees. Capping liability for data incidents at the same level as general service issues leaves you exposed
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to either exclude data breaches from liability caps entirely or establish a separate, higher cap specifically for data protection violations (e.g., €10M or 10x annual fees)

clauses.finding: Provider must delete or return all personal data within 30 days of termination
clauses.explanation: Ensures data is not retained indefinitely after the relationship ends, reducing ongoing exposure and supporting GDPR data minimization principles
clauses.recommendation: Request written certification of deletion and clarify whether 'return' means secure transfer or destruction after Customer receives it

clauses.finding: Governed by laws of Ireland with disputes subject to GDPR requirements
clauses.explanation: Ireland is an EU member with strong data protection oversight, and explicit GDPR reference is appropriate. However, forum for disputes is not specified
clauses.risk_recommendation: Verify Irish law is acceptable for your organization and clarify whether disputes will be handled in Irish courts or through arbitration

red_flags: Liability for data breaches is capped by the main agreement, potentially leaving significant exposure uncovered for what could be multi-million dollar incidents
Security measures are described only at high level with no specific standards, certifications, or audit rights mentioned
No mention of data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure requests) or how Provider will assist Customer in fulfilling these GDPR obligations
Missing provisions for international data transfers if Provider or sub-processors are outside the EU/EEA
No specification of Provider's insurance coverage for data breach incidents
top_recommendation: Negotiate to carve out data breaches and GDPR violations from the liability cap in the main agreement, or establish a separate minimum liability cap of at least €5-10 million for data protection failures before signing.

plain_english_summary: This is a straightforward sponsorship agreement where a sponsor pays $25,000 for Gold Tier benefits at an annual conference, including booth space, passes, and a speaking slot. The contract includes reasonable protections for both parties with liability caps, cancellation refunds, and force majeure provisions.

clauses.finding: Payment split 50% on signing and 50% due 30 days before event
clauses.explanation: Standard payment structure for event sponsorships that balances cash flow for organizer with some protection for sponsor by delaying half the payment until closer to event
clauses.recommendation: Acceptable as written; ensures sponsor has some leverage if issues arise before final payment

clauses.finding: Full refund or credit at sponsor's choice if organizer cancels event
clauses.explanation: Protects the sponsor's investment if the organizer cannot deliver the event, giving flexibility to choose refund or future credit
clauses.recommendation: This is favorable to sponsor; no changes needed

clauses.finding: Liability capped at sponsorship amount ($25,000) with no indirect damages for either party
clauses.explanation: Both parties are protected from catastrophic losses and can only recover the actual sponsorship fee paid, which is reasonable for this type of agreement
clauses.recommendation: Standard and balanced provision; acceptable for a $25,000 sponsorship

clauses.finding: Limited mutual logo use for event purposes only, following provided guidelines
clauses.explanation: Clearly restricts brand usage to the specific event context, preventing either party from misusing the other's trademark beyond the agreed scope
clauses.recommendation: Good protection for both brands; ensure brand guidelines are actually provided and followed

clauses.finding: Neither party liable for events beyond control; pro rata refund of fees
clauses.explanation: Provides fair protection if unforeseen circumstances prevent the event, with proportional refund rather than full loss to sponsor
clauses.recommendation: Reasonable provision especially post-pandemic; clarify what 'pro rata' means based on timing of cancellation

clauses.finding: Tennessee law governs the agreement
clauses.explanation: Standard choice of law provision; Tennessee is a reasonable jurisdiction with predictable contract law
clauses.recommendation: Acceptable unless sponsor is located far from Tennessee and prefers local jurisdiction; consider adding dispute resolution method

red_flags: No specific date or details about the 'annual conference' - event date, location, and year should be clearly identified
Missing termination rights for sponsor if organizer materially breaches (e.g., fails to provide promised benefits)
No dispute resolution mechanism specified (arbitration, mediation, or litigation venue)
Effective Date referenced but not defined in the signature block
No clear definition of what benefits constitute 'Gold Tier' or process if promised benefits are not delivered
top_recommendation: Add specific event details (date, location, year) and define what happens if the organizer fails to deliver the promised sponsorship benefits before accepting this agreement

plain_english_summary: This is a straightforward month-to-month consulting agreement for 20 hours of business advisory services at $4,000 per month. The terms are generally balanced and fair to both parties, with standard protections and an easy exit clause requiring only 30 days notice.

clauses.finding: Consultant provides up to 20 hours per month of business advisory services
clauses.explanation: The scope is clear but 'business advisory services' is somewhat vague and could lead to disputes about what's included or excluded
clauses.recommendation: Consider adding a brief list of specific services or examples to avoid scope creep or misunderstandings

clauses.finding: Monthly retainer of $4,000 with unused hours rolling over for one month and additional hours at $200/hour
clauses.explanation: Clear pricing structure with rollover provision that's fair to the client; the hourly rate for additional work is reasonable and transparent
clauses.recommendation: Ensure payment terms (due date, method) are clarified in writing or invoice terms

clauses.finding: Month-to-month agreement with 30 days written notice required from either party
clauses.explanation: This provides excellent flexibility for both parties to exit the relationship without long-term commitment or penalties
clauses.recommendation: No changes needed; this is a business-friendly term

clauses.finding: Both parties must keep confidential information private for 2 years
clauses.explanation: Standard mutual confidentiality protection, though 2 years is relatively short for sensitive business information
clauses.recommendation: Consider extending to 3-5 years if dealing with highly sensitive trade secrets or strategic information

clauses.finding: Client owns deliverables upon payment; Consultant retains general methodologies
clauses.explanation: Clear work-product ownership for the client while allowing consultant to reuse their general tools and methods with other clients
clauses.recommendation: Ensure 'general methodologies' is understood by both parties to avoid future disputes

clauses.finding: Consultant's liability is capped at fees paid in the prior 3 months
clauses.explanation: This limits the consultant's maximum exposure to $12,000, which may be insufficient if their advice causes significant financial harm to the business
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to remove or increase the cap, or ensure adequate professional liability insurance covers the consultant

clauses.finding: Consultant classified as independent contractor responsible for own taxes
clauses.explanation: Standard classification that protects both parties from employment-related obligations and tax withholding issues
clauses.recommendation: Client should ensure consultant provides W-9 and receives 1099 at year-end for IRS compliance

clauses.finding: Agreement governed by laws of North Carolina
clauses.explanation: Establishes clear jurisdiction for any disputes; no indication whether this favors either party
clauses.recommendation: Confirm both parties are comfortable with North Carolina law and consider adding dispute resolution method (mediation/arbitration)

red_flags: Limitation of liability caps consultant exposure at only 3 months of fees ($12,000), which may be inadequate if bad advice causes significant business losses
No indemnification clause means neither party is protected if the other's actions cause third-party claims
Vague service description ('business advisory services') could lead to scope disputes
Short 2-year confidentiality period may not adequately protect long-term sensitive information
top_recommendation: Negotiate to increase or remove the liability cap in Section 6, and consider adding mutual indemnification protection for third-party claims

plain_english_summary: This is an extremely one-sided supply agreement that exposes the Buyer to massive financial and legal risk. The Buyer has virtually no protections, must pay for the Supplier's defects and legal claims, is locked into purchasing 100,000 units annually for 3 years with no quality guarantees, while the Supplier can walk away anytime and raise prices at will.

clauses.finding: Supplier can increase prices with only 10 days notice; Buyer must accept or forfeit all pending orders
clauses.explanation: Supplier has complete pricing control with minimal notice. If you reject a price increase, you lose all orders already placed, which could devastate your production schedule and leave you scrambling for alternatives.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate a price ceiling, annual increase cap (e.g., CPI + 2%), or right to terminate without penalty if prices increase beyond a threshold.

clauses.finding: All products sold AS-IS with no warranties of merchantability or fitness for purpose
clauses.explanation: You have zero guarantee that products will work or be suitable for your needs. Even if they're completely defective, you have no recourse based on implied warranties that normally protect buyers.
clauses.recommendation: Demand at minimum a limited warranty covering defects in materials and workmanship for a reasonable period (90-180 days). This is standard in commercial supply contracts.

clauses.finding: Supplier's liability capped at the price of a single defective unit, even for total product recalls
clauses.explanation: If 100,000 units are defective and cause a recall costing millions, Supplier only pays for one unit. You absorb all recall costs, customer damages, and business losses. This is catastrophically inadequate.
clauses.recommendation: Require liability cap at minimum equal to 12 months of purchases or the value of defective shipment. Product recalls and consequential damages should have separate, higher caps.

clauses.finding: Buyer must defend and indemnify Supplier for ALL product claims, even those caused by Supplier's own manufacturing defects
clauses.explanation: This is backwards and unconscionable. If Supplier manufactures defective products that injure someone, YOU must pay Supplier's legal bills and any damages. You're insuring Supplier against their own negligence.
clauses.recommendation: Completely reject this clause. Standard indemnification should be mutual, with each party covering claims arising from their own negligence or breach. Supplier must indemnify you for manufacturing defects.

clauses.finding: Buyer commits to purchasing minimum 100,000 units annually or must pay the full shortfall amount
clauses.explanation: You're locked into buying 100,000 units per year regardless of your actual needs, product quality, or market conditions. If demand drops or products are defective, you still owe the full purchase price for units you don't want.
clauses.recommendation: Reduce minimum commitment significantly, add force majeure exceptions, and make commitment contingent on product quality meeting specifications. Consider a tiered structure with lower penalties.

clauses.finding: Supplier can terminate for convenience with 30 days notice; Buyer locked in for 3-year minimum term
clauses.explanation: Supplier can walk away anytime with one month notice, leaving you scrambling for a new manufacturer. Meanwhile, you're trapped for 3 years even if quality deteriorates, prices skyrocket, or you find better alternatives.
clauses.recommendation: Demand mutual termination rights: either both parties can terminate for convenience with equal notice (90-180 days), or neither can. Include termination rights for material breach and quality failures.

clauses.finding: Contract governed by laws of a jurisdiction designated by Supplier
clauses.explanation: Supplier chooses the legal jurisdiction unilaterally, potentially selecting one inconvenient or unfavorable to you. This increases litigation costs and may apply laws more favorable to suppliers.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate mutual agreement on jurisdiction, preferably your home jurisdiction or a neutral location. At minimum, specify the actual jurisdiction in the contract rather than leaving it to Supplier's discretion.

red_flags: Buyer must indemnify Supplier even for Supplier's own manufacturing defects - completely unreasonable risk transfer
Liability capped at single unit price even for mass recalls affecting 100,000+ units
No product warranties whatsoever - AS-IS sale with no quality guarantees
Asymmetric termination: Supplier exits anytime, Buyer locked in for 3 years with 100K unit annual minimum
Unilateral pricing power with 10-day notice and forfeiture penalty
Supplier unilaterally selects governing law jurisdiction
top_recommendation: Do not sign this agreement under any circumstances. The indemnification clause alone could bankrupt your company if defective products cause harm. Engage legal counsel to completely restructure this contract with balanced risk allocation, meaningful warranties, mutual termination rights, and standard supplier liability provisions before proceeding.

plain_english_summary: This is a marketing services agreement for social media and paid advertising management at $6,000/month plus ad spend costs. The contract auto-renews annually with 60-day notice required to cancel, and the agency's financial liability is capped at the last 3 months of fees while they don't guarantee any specific marketing results.

clauses.finding: Contract automatically renews for 12-month terms unless either party provides 60 days advance notice
clauses.explanation: You could be locked into another full year if you miss the 60-day notice window. This is easy to overlook and could trap you in an underperforming relationship for 12 more months.
clauses.recommendation: Set a calendar reminder 75 days before each renewal date. Consider negotiating for 30-day notice or month-to-month terms after the initial period.

clauses.finding: Agency liability capped at fees paid in the prior 3 months, excluding ad spend
clauses.explanation: If the agency mismanages your ad campaigns and wastes your ad budget, you can only recover up to $18,000 (3 months × $6,000) even if ad spend losses are much higher. The ad spend exclusion is particularly problematic.
clauses.recommendation: Negotiate to include ad spend in the liability cap, especially if you're planning significant advertising budgets. Consider a minimum cap of $50,000-$100,000 or 12 months of fees.

clauses.finding: Agency explicitly does not guarantee any specific results; all metrics are targets only
clauses.explanation: You have no contractual recourse if the agency fails to deliver meaningful results. They could perform poorly indefinitely without breaching the contract.
clauses.recommendation: Add performance-based KPIs with minimum thresholds (e.g., engagement rates, conversion metrics) that trigger review meetings or early termination rights if consistently missed.

clauses.finding: Client owns final approved creative assets upon payment; Agency retains templates and analytics tools
clauses.explanation: You'll own the finished work you pay for, but the agency keeps their internal processes and tools. This is standard, though 'upon payment' means unpaid invoices could create ownership disputes.
clauses.recommendation: Ensure timely payment to secure ownership. Clarify what happens to assets if you terminate mid-project with partial payment.

clauses.finding: Monthly fee of $6,000 plus ad spend at cost, invoices due net 15
clauses.explanation: Pricing structure is clear with ad spend passed through at cost. The 15-day payment term is relatively tight but manageable for most businesses.
clauses.recommendation: Request net 30 terms if your cash flow cycle requires it. Confirm how ad spend is documented and that you receive transparent reporting on all charges.

clauses.finding: Both parties must protect confidential information during the term and for 2 years after
clauses.explanation: Standard mutual protection that safeguards your business information, marketing strategies, and customer data. The 2-year post-termination period is reasonable.
clauses.recommendation: Ensure your proprietary customer lists, marketing strategies, and business data are clearly marked as confidential when shared.

clauses.finding: Contract is governed by Utah state law
clauses.explanation: Utah law will apply to any disputes. This is only a concern if you're located far from Utah and would prefer your own state's laws or if Utah has unfavorable consumer protection laws.
clauses.recommendation: If you're not in Utah, consider negotiating for your home state's law or adding that disputes will be handled in your jurisdiction to avoid travel costs.

clauses.finding: No explicit termination for cause or convenience clause beyond the 60-day renewal notice
clauses.explanation: You may be locked in for the full 12-month term with no ability to exit early even if the agency performs poorly or your business needs change. This creates significant inflexibility.
clauses.recommendation: Add a termination for convenience clause allowing either party to exit with 30-60 days notice and pro-rated final payment. Include immediate termination rights for material breach.

red_flags: Auto-renewal with 60-day notice requirement could trap you in underperforming relationship for another year
Liability cap excludes ad spend, meaning wasted advertising budget cannot be recovered beyond the $18,000 fee cap
No performance guarantees or minimum standards means agency has no accountability for results
Missing termination for cause or convenience provisions may lock you into full 12-month terms
top_recommendation: Before signing, negotiate for: (1) termination for convenience with 30-60 days notice, (2) liability cap that includes ad spend or is set at a reasonable minimum threshold, and (3) performance metrics with review triggers that allow early exit if targets are consistently missed.
